assumptions: Please act as a professional verilog designer.
Implement a 64-bit Johnson counter (torsional ring counter), and the state of the similar 4-bit Johnson counter example is as follows: 0000, 1000, 1100, 1110, 1111, 0111, 0011, 0001, 0000.
On every rising edge of the clock signal (clk) or the falling edge of the reset signal (rst_n), the counter value is updated.
If the reset signal (rst_n) is low, indicating a reset condition, the counter value (Q) is set to 0.
Otherwise, if the least significant bit (Q[0]) is 0, the counter increments by shifting the current value (Q[63:1]) to the right and appending a 1 at the most significant bit position.
If the least significant bit (Q[0]) is 1, the counter decrements by shifting the current value (Q[63:1]) to the right and appending a 0 at the most significant bit position.
Task description was provided and only used as a low-risk planning hint.
Spec text was provided and only used where it did not contradict the contract.

expected_properties: Observed outputs or protocol-visible signals settle to a stable, non-X post-reset state.
No fixed-cycle completion is assumed during reset recovery.
cases.goal: Establish a stable post-reset baseline before functional checking.
notes: Reset polarity may still be heuristic if the contract marks it ambiguous.
preconditions: Reset signal is available in the contract.
stimulus_intent: Assert the detected reset using the inferred polarity.
Release reset conservatively and observe interface stabilization.
timing_assumptions: Do not assume completion before it becomes externally visible.

expected_properties: A legal operation causes an observable state or output change after conservative observation.
The plan does not assume a completion event before it is externally visible.
When rst_n is high and a rising edge of clk occurs, Q updates according to the Johnson rule based on the current LSB.
Q remains stable between clock edges.
cases.goal: Validate correct single‑step state transition of the 64‑bit Johnson counter.
notes: Case intent is conservative when the contract is weak or timing is unresolved.
Observation is performed conservatively after the next rising edge.
preconditions: Use only ports and observable signals present in the contract.
stimulus_intent: Drive a representative legal input pattern across known inputs: ['clk', 'rst_n']
Toggle clk once while keeping rst_n deasserted to trigger one Johnson update.
timing_assumptions: Do not assume completion before it becomes externally visible.
Observe Q at the next rising edge after the stimulus; no fixed‑latency assumption beyond clocked visibility.

The second operation is observed only through externally visible acceptance, completion, or output change.

expected_properties: When rst_n is asserted low while clk is idle, Q remains at its pre‑reset value and does not exhibit spurious transitions.
cases.goal: Verify that asserting the active‑low reset without clock activity does not cause unintended Q changes.
notes: Reset polarity for clk is ambiguous; this case avoids relying on any clk edge during reset.
preconditions: rst_n can be driven low while clk is held steady.
stimulus_intent: Hold clk low, assert rst_n low for multiple cycles, then deassert rst_n and observe Q stability.
timing_assumptions: Allow at least two clock periods after deasserting reset before sampling Q.

expected_properties: The least‑significant bit Q[0] toggles on every rising edge of clk when reset is not asserted.
Q[63] follows the Johnson rule: it becomes 1 when Q[0] was 0, and 0 when Q[0] was 1.
cases.goal: Validate the characteristic Johnson‑counter state evolution across consecutive cycles without assuming a fixed latency.
notes: Checks relational properties rather than exact numeric values, preserving ambiguity in timing.
preconditions: Counter is released from reset and runs for several clock cycles.
stimulus_intent: Generate a burst of rising edges on clk while keeping rst_n deasserted.
timing_assumptions: Sample Q after each rising edge; no assumption of exact cycle count for full period.
